Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

  "opinion": "As a 73-year-old self-employed electrician living in Rome with a vocational diploma, I’ve spent decades building trust through hands-on work and community. I’ve noticed that social media can be a double-edged sword—useful for staying connected with family and friends but also vulnerable to misinformation. My approach starts with a simple but powerful principle: if it feels too good to be true, it’s probably fake. When I see posts about health tips or financial advice that seem too perfect, I pause and check multiple sources, just like I’d cross-reference measurements before wiring a circuit. I also limit my time on social media to 30 minutes a day, focusing on platforms where I know the community is active and reliable, such as Facebook groups for local artisans and hobbyists. My faith in Christianity guides me to seek truth with patience and humility, so I don’t react impulsively to viral content. I’ve found that sharing my own experiences—like how I learned to bake better pastries through YouTube tutorials—builds trust more than criticizing others. For older adults like me, staying informed means being selective, not isolated. I encourage my children and grandchildren to ask questions when they see something confusing, just as I used to ask my electrician colleagues for advice. Ultimately, managing fake content isn’t about avoiding it but about building a mindset of careful, compassionate verification. This keeps me grounded in reality, much like checking the voltage before touching a wire.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a 73-year-old self-employed electrician living in Rome with a vocational diploma, I’ve spent decades building trust through hands-on work and community. I’ve noticed that social media can be a double-edged sword—useful for staying connected with family and friends but also vulnerable to misinformation. My approach starts with a simple but powerful principle: if it feels too good to be true, it’s probably fake. When I see posts about health tips or financial advice that seem too perfect, I pause and check multiple sources, just like I’d cross-reference measurements before wiring a circuit. I also limit my time on social media to 30 minutes a day, focusing on platforms where I know the community is active and reliable, such as Facebook groups for local artisans and hobbyists. My faith in Christianity guides me to seek truth with patience and humility, so I don’t react impulsively to viral content. I’ve found that sharing my own experiences—like how I learned to bake better pastries through YouTube tutorials—builds trust more than criticizing others. For older adults like me, staying informed means being selective, not isolated. I encourage my children and grandchildren to ask questions when they see something confusing, just as I used to ask my electrician colleagues for advice. Ultimately, managing fake content isn’t about avoiding it but about building a mindset of careful, compassionate verification. This keeps me grounded in reality, much like checking the voltage before touching a wire.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 73-year-old self-employed electrician in Rome with a vocational diploma. They have good mental health, Christian beliefs, and spend several hours daily on social media. Their low openness and low neuroticism suggest they prefer structured, reliable information. The opinion draws on their practical background (electrician), faith (Christianity), and hobbies (baking, rock climbing) to emphasize verification, time limits, and community-based trust. The tone is calm, practical, and grounded in lived experience.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, practical, grounded in lived experience
citations: Electrician's experience with verification and safety checks
